request.question: Where are the studios, where the Muppet Shows were recorded, located ?

title: Michelan Sisti
The Muppet Show
Muppet*Vision 3D
Babilla (album)
Eamonn Andrews Studios
Countdown (game show)
WFAA Communications Center Studios
BBC Elstree Centre
De Lane Lea Studios
Mama Melrose's Ristorante Italiano
sentences: Michelan Sisti (born May 27, 1949) is a Puerto Rican-American actor, director, puppeteer, and musician who played Michelangelo in "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles" and its sequel "".
He previously had an eighteen-year theatrical career including five Broadway shows.
Since "TMNT", Micha moved from New York City to Los Angeles and has continued to work with The Jim Henson Company, Walt Disney Studios, and many other movie and television productions.
September 8–10, 2017, he was an additional Muppet performer for a live show at the Hollywood Bowl titled "The Muppets Take the Bowl".
The Muppet Show is a family-oriented comedy-variety television series that was produced by puppeteer Jim Henson and features The Muppets.

After two pilot episodes produced in 1974 and 1975 failed to get the attention of network executives in the United States, Lew Grade approached Henson to produce the programme in the United Kingdom for ATV.

The show lasted for five series consisting of 120 episodes which were first broadcast in Britain between 5 September 1976 and 15 March 1981 on ATV and was shown by the other ITV franchises in the United Kingdom.
The programs were recorded at Elstree Studios, England.
Muppet*Vision 3D is a 3D film attraction located at Disney's Hollywood Studios.

Directed by Jim Henson, the attraction features Kermit the Frog guiding park guests on a tour through Muppet Studios, while the Muppets prepare their sketch acts to demonstrate their new breakthrough in 3D film technology.
The show, however, completely unravels when Dr. Bunsen Honeydew's experimental 3D sprite, Waldo, causes mayhem during the next portion of the show.
Babilla is the thirteenth studio album by Puerto Rican rapper Vico C, released on October 6, 2009 through Caribbean Records under exclusive license to EMI Televisa.

The 69% of the album was recorded at Luny Tunes' Más Flow Studios in Carolina, Puerto Rico, while the remaining 31% was recorded at Pichaera Studios, Golden Boy Studios (both in San Juan, Puerto Rico) and Rotary Music Studios (Bayamón, Puerto Rico).
Vico C produced almost the entire album, while Hyde, Luis Almonte and Joel Morales worked as recording engineers.
Half the album was mixed at KDS Studios, while American producer Mike Fuller mastered it at Fullersound Inc., both studios located in Florida.
All tracks feature session musicians for guitars, drums, trumpet, trombone, saxophone and choirs, while Vico C played the electronic keyboard on eight of the thirteen songs that includes the album.
Eamonn Andrews Studios was founded by broadcaster Eamonn Andrews, famous for being the presenter with the big red book of "This Is Your Life," a British television programme.
In the 1960s, the launch of television in Ireland led him to become the Chairman of the RTÉ Authority.

He set up a media production company in Ireland, the Eamonn Andrews Studios, which ran a dance hall called The Television Club in Harcourt Street in Dublin and took on the lease of the Gaiety Theatre in Dublin.
Eamonn Andrews Studios were originally located at 40 Henry Street in Dublin.
In the 1960s the company bought the TV Club building on Harcourt Street.
It was located directly opposite, what was then, the Old Harcourt Street Station.
It housed two distinctly different studios.
A radio studio which recorded sponsored programmes for Radio Éireann, and commercials, and a band studio down stairs.
It used the dance hall during the day to record all the main bands of the Showband era.
At nighttime, the bands were showcased live on stage in what the company called "The Mecca of Irish Nightlife".
Countdown is a British game show involving word and number puzzles.
It is produced by ITV Studios and broadcast on Channel 4.
It is currently presented by Nick Hewer, assisted by Rachel Riley, with regular lexicographer Susie Dent.
It was the first programme to be aired on Channel 4, and 77 series have been broadcast since its debut on 2 November 1982.

With over 6,500 episodes, "Countdown" is one of the longest-running game shows in the world, along with the original French version, "Des chiffres et des lettres" (Numbers & Letters), which has been running on French television continuously since 1965.
"Countdown" was initially recorded at The Leeds Studios for 27 years, before moving to Granada Studios in 2009, and then over to MediaCityUK in Salford Quays in 2013.
WFAA Communications Center Studios are the main studio operations of Tegna, Inc. owned television station WFAA, which is an ABC affiliate, located at 606 Young Street in Dallas, Texas.
WFAA-TV is the only station in the Dallas-Fort Worth broadcasting market to not become a network O&O station.
The studio opened in 1961, and housed WFAA-TV, WFAA-AM, and WFAA-FM, later known as KZEW-FM.
From 1938 to 1970, WFAA-AM was both an NBC and ABC station which meant that the building housed both the NBC and ABC networks.
The station that WFAA shared networks with was WBAP-AM which was housed at Broadcast Hill in Fort Worth.
In the early years, WFAA was more known to Dallas and WBAP was more known to Fort Worth.
WFAA-TV played a strong part in youthful programs such as Sump'n Else, The Group And Chapman, Peppermint Place, and many others.
During the 1960s, WFAA-AM continued to broadcast entertainment, but its ratings were weakened due to Top 40 station KLIF-AM's format.
The KLIF Triangle Point Studios are located a few blocks from WFAA in Downtown Dallas.
WFAA Studios are a major part of Dallas-Fort Worth broadcasting history.
At one time, Downtown Dallas had three different broadcast headquarters, which are WFAA, KRLD, and KLIF.
While many national television shows were on tour, they occasionally filmed a few segments at WFAA Studios.
BBC Elstree Centre, sometimes referred to as BBC Elstree Studios, is a television production facility located on Clarendon Road in Borehamwood, Hertfordshire.
De Lane Lea Studios is a recording studio, currently based in Dean Street, Soho, London, England, UK.

Although the studios have mainly been used for dubbing feature films and television programmes, major artists such as The Animals, the Beatles, Soft Machine, Queen, the Rolling Stones, Bee Gees, The Who, the Jimi Hendrix Experience, Pink Floyd, Renaissance, Electric Light Orchestra and Deep Purple recorded songs in their studios, particularly at their former premises at 129 Kingsway  and at Engineers Way, Wembley, where Queen recorded demos in 1971.
After De Lane Lea, these studios were taken over by Lee Lighting which then, many years later, became the Fountain Studios.
It is now home to shows like "The X Factor".
Mama Melrose's Ristorante Italiano is a restaurant in the Grand Avenue area at Disney's Hollywood Studios.
Located near Muppet*Vision 3D and Star Tours, the restaurant specializes in Italian cuisine, serving such dishes as "ossobuco", wood-fired flatbread pizza, and grilled salmon.
A "Fantasmic!
" dinner package is available that grants restaurant guests quicker access to this show.
This package is also offered at two other restaurants in the park: the Hollywood Brown Derby and Hollywood & Vine.
Ron Douglas included the recipe for Mama Melrose's cappuccino "crème brûlée" in his cookbook "America's Most Wanted Recipes: Just Desserts".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Where are the studios, where the Muppet Shows were recorded, located ?
hop1_docs: Sam Eagle | Sam Eagle is a Muppet character originating from the television show "The Muppet Show", where he was performed by Frank Oz.  Sam has appeared in every Muppet film; as himself in "The Muppet Movie", "The Great Muppet Caper", "The Muppets Take Manhattan", "Muppets from Space", and "The Muppets", as well as the Head Schoolmaster in "The Muppet Christmas Carol," Samuel Arrow in "Muppet Treasure Island" and a CIA agent in "Muppets Most Wanted."  He also appears in the television series, "The Muppets."

The Muppet Show | The Muppet Show is a family-oriented comedy-variety television series that was produced by puppeteer Jim Henson and features The Muppets.  After two pilot episodes produced in 1974 and 1975 failed to get the attention of network executives in the United States, Lew Grade approached Henson to produce the programme in the United Kingdom for ATV.  The show lasted for five series consisting of 120 episodes which were first broadcast in Britain between 5 September 1976 and 15 March 1981 on ATV and was shown by the other ITV franchises in the United Kingdom.  The programs were recorded at Elstree Studios, England.

Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem | Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem is a Muppet rock house band that debuted on "The Muppet Show".  Following "The Muppet Show", they appeared in various Muppet movies and television specials and have also recorded album tracks and covered numerous songs.  Dr. Teeth and Animal were designed by Jim Henson while the rest of the original band members were designed by Michael K. Frith.  They made their debut in 1975's "" and the pilot for "The Muppet Show".

The Muppets discography | The discography of The Muppets, an American puppet group originally created by puppeteer Jim Henson, includes of fifteen studio albums, four compilation albums, one extended play, eight singles, two "featured artist" singles and five music videos.  Of the studio and compilation albums, three are soundtracks from seasons of "The Muppet Show" and seven of eight motion pictures.  The soundtrack to the sixth film, "Muppets from Space" was orchestral as opposed to derivative compositions performed by the characters.  Walt Disney Records has served as the primary record label for musical projects by The Muppets since 2004.

Muppet*Vision 3D | Muppet*Vision 3D is a 3D film attraction located at Disney's Hollywood Studios.  Directed by Jim Henson, the attraction features Kermit the Frog guiding park guests on a tour through Muppet Studios, while the Muppets prepare their sketch acts to demonstrate their new breakthrough in 3D film technology.  The show, however, completely unravels when Dr. Bunsen Honeydew's experimental 3D sprite, Waldo, causes mayhem during the next portion of the show.

Gonzo (Muppet) | Developed and performed by Dave Goelz, Gonzo made his first appearance in the 1970 special "The Great Santa Claus Switch", as the "Cigar Box Frackle".  Originally a minor figure in "The Muppet Show", he soon evolved into one of the franchise's primary characters.  Gonzo has appeared in every Muppet film, including "The Muppet Christmas Carol", where he portrayed author Charles Dickens and developed a double act with Rizzo the Rat.

The Muppet Show (comics) | The Muppet Show is comic book based on the variety television series "The Muppet Show" and featuring The Muppets.  The series was written and drawn by Roger Langridge and published by Boom Kids! , an imprint of Boom!  Studios.  In 2011, the Boom!  license with Disney Publishing Worldwide expired.  Disney's own comic book publishing subsidiary, Marvel Comics, renamed the series "Muppets" and published four issues in 2012.
hop1_titles: Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "The Muppet Show" page (passage [2]) states the programs were "recorded at Elstree Studios, England." (quote: "recorded at Elstree Studios, England.")  
- The attraction page "Muppet*Vision 3D" (passage [5]) uses the term "Muppet Studios" (quote: "Muppet Studios"), which is a different, fictional/attraction name.

2) Ambiguities:
- Question uses "Muppet Shows" (plural); passages provide "The Muppet Show" (singular) as the relevant page (passage [2]). Do not assume plurality implies a different page.
- "Elstree Studios" may have multiple related pages/variants (e.g., studio complex vs. specific studio site); no Elstree Studios page/variant is included among the provided passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as given in passages): "Elstree Studios".
- Precise missing fact needed to complete the answer: the canonical geographic location details for Elstree Studios (official town and county, and confirmation that this is the studio complex where The Muppet Show was filmed). Passage [2] gives "England" but the exact town/county and the authoritative studio-page confirmation are not present.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Fetch the "Elstree Studios" page (open lede/infobox and 'Location' or 'History' sections) to get the official town/county and confirm it is the studio used for The Muppet Show. (Search: "Elstree Studios location lede infobox 'located in'")
- Re-open "The Muppet Show" page (check lede/Production/Recording/Infobox) to verify the recording statement and any citations pointing to the studio page. (Search: "The Muppet Show recorded at Elstree Studios production")
- If multiple Elstree pages exist, fetch "Elstree Studios (disambiguation)" or pages like "Elstree Film Studios" / "Elstree and Borehamwood studios" to resolve which specific facility is meant. (Search: "Elstree Studios disambiguation Elstree Borehamwood")

Do not provide the final answer yet—final response should wait until those pages/sections are retrieved and checked.
hop2_docs: Elstree Studios | Elstree Studios is a generic term which can refer to several current and defunct British film studios and television studios based in or around the towns of Borehamwood and Elstree in Hertfordshire.  Studios have been located here since film production began in the area during 1914.

Borehamwood | Borehamwood ( —originally spelt Boreham Wood), is a town in southern Hertfordshire.  It is a commuter town near Watford and London, situated 12 mi from Charing Cross.  Borehamwood has a population of 31,074, and is within the civil parish of Elstree and Borehamwood and the London commuter belt.  There is one weekly newspaper, the "Borehamwood and Elstree Times".  The town is perhaps most well known for its film and TV studios, commonly known as Elstree Studios, hence the association with Elstree.
BBC Elstree Centre | BBC Elstree Centre, sometimes referred to as BBC Elstree Studios, is a television production facility located on Clarendon Road in Borehamwood, Hertfordshire.

Elstree Studios (Shenley Road) | Elstree Studios on Shenley Road, Borehamwood, Hertfordshire is a British film and television production facility operated by Elstree Film Studios Limited.  One of several facilities historically referred to as Elstree Studios, the Shenley Road studio site opened in 1925.

Elstree and Borehamwood | Elstree and Borehamwood is a civil parish in the Borough of Hertsmere in Hertfordshire, England.  Located approximately 12 mi northwest of central London and adjacent to the Greater London boundary, it is an urbanised parish with suburban residential development, some open land and light industry.  Elstree and Borehamwood is a recent renaming of the ancient parish of Elstree, covering the settlements of Elstree and Borehamwood.  Formed in 1894 as the Elstree Parish Council, the local council is Elstree and Borehamwood Town Council.  One of the most populous civil parishes in England, at the 2011 census it had a population of 37,065.

Elstree University Technical College | The Elstree University Technical College (shortened to EUTC) is a university technical college located in Borehamwood, Hertfordshire, England, which opened in September 2013.  The college's lead sponsor is the University of Hertfordshire; other sponsors include Elstree Studios.  The UTC occupies newly developed buildings on Elstree Way.  The conversion was designed by Ellis Williams Architects and was undertaken by Willmott Dixon Interiors.

Gate Studios | Gate Studios formed a part of Elstree Studios in the town of Borehamwood, UK, Opened in 1928, the studios were in use until the early 1950s.  The studios had previously been known as Whitehall Studios, Consolidated Studios, J.H. Studios and M.P. Studios.
response.summary_2: "recorded at Elstree Studios, England." Elstree Studios is a generic term for several British film and television studios based in and around the towns of Borehamwood and Elstree in Hertfordshire.
response.answer: Elstree Studios — in and around the towns of Borehamwood and Elstree in Hertfordshire, England.
